current.title: 1×はYです "I am a student." "My major is the Japanese language." "It is 12:30": These sentences will all be translated into Japanese using an appropriate noun and the word desu. ~です。 It is ... がくせいです。 (1) am a student. Gakusee desu. にほんごです。 (My major) is the Japanese language. Nihongo desu. じゅうにじはんです。 (It) is half past twelve. Juuniji han desu. Note that none of these sentences has a "subject," like the "I," "my major," and "it" found in their English counterparts. Sentences without subjects are very common in Japanese; Japanese speakers actually tend to omit subjects whenever they think it is clear to the listener what or who they are referring to. If it is not clear from the background situation or the preceding context what is being talked about, you can start a sentence with a "topic" marked by wa. Note that when we write in hiragana, we use the letter It for topic wa following the classical orthography.'

current.text: 1 The hiragana t therefore has two pronunciations: wa (in the topic position) and ha (in most other positions). There are few exceptions, such as konnichiwa (good afternoon) and k…

current.title: 42会話・文法编 Wa is a member of the class of words called "particles." So is the word no, which we will turn to later in this lesson. We add particles to noun phrases to indicate how the phrases relate to the rest of the sentence. Note also that nouns like gakusee and sensee in the above examples stand alone, unlike their Eng- lish translations "student" and "teacher," which are preceded by "a." In Japanese, there is no item that corresponds to "a," nor is there any item that corresponds to the plural "-s" at the end of a noun. Without context, a sentence like gakusee desu is therefore ambiguous in terms of the singu- lar and plural interpretations.

current.title: 3 Noun1の Nounz No is a particle that connects two nouns. The phrase Sakura daigaku no gakusee means "a student at Sakura University" The second noun gakusee provides the main idea" (being a student) and the first one Sakura daigaku makes it more specific (not a high school, but a college student). You can use no like the possessive ("Takeshi's") in English, as in the first example below. Here are some examples of no between two nouns. The main idea is always the nounz, with the noun, providing restrictions, specifications, etc.

current.title: 表現ノート ひよう lfhu あのAnoo indicates that you have some reservations about saying what you are going to say next. You may be worried about interrupting something some- one is currently doing, or sounding rude and impolite for asking personal ques- tions, for example. そうですSoo desu ka acknowledges that you have understood what was just said. "Is that so?" (with rising intonation) or "I see" (with falling intonation). NumbersMany number words have more than one pronunciation. Refer to the table on pp. 380-381 for a general picture. 0 ぜ口and vare both commonly used. 1 ち, but pronounced as いinいぶん (one minute) andいい (one year old). 2 l all the time. When you are reading out each digit separately, as when you give your phone number, it may be pronounced with a long vowel, asにい. 3 さん all the time. The part that follows it may change in sound, as in んぶん, insteadofさんふん. 4 よ ん is the most basic, but fourth-year student is よ ね んtv and four o'clock is よ C. In some combinations that we will later learn, it is read as L (as in Lが, April). The part that follows this number may change in sound too, as in よ hぶん. 5 all the time. When read out separately, it may be pronounced with a long vowel, as う. 6 ろく，but pronounced as ろつ inろっぶん. 7 なな is the most basic, but seven o'clock is Lち じ. 8はち, but usually pronounced as はっin はっぶん and はっさい. 9きう is the most basic, but nine o'clock isくじ. 10 じゅ，but pronounced as じゆっorじっinじゆっぶん/じっぶん andじっさいじっさい. せhtl\The word sensee is usually reserved for describing somebody else's oc- cupation. Watashi wa sensee desu makes sense, but may sound slightly arrogant, because the word sensee actually means an "honorable master." If you (or a mem- ber of your family) are a teacher, and if you want to be really modest, you can use the word kyooshi instead.

current.title: 第1課45 会 L1 さhSan is placed after a name as a generic title. It goes both with a given name and a family name. Children are referred to as chan (and boys in particular as kun), rather than san. Professors, doctors, lawyers and others in "high-status oc- cupations" are usually referred to with the title sensee rather than san. San and other title words are never used in reference to oneself. Referring to the person you are talking toThe word for "you," anata, is not very commonly used in Japanese. Instead, we use the name and a title like san and sensee to refer to the person you are talking to. Therefore, a sentence like "Ms.

current.title: Culture Notes にほんじんのなまえJapanese Names Nihonjin no namae When Japanese give their name, they say their family name first and given name last (middle names do not exist). When introducing themselves, they often say only their family name. Here are some typical Japanese names. Given name Family name Women Men ゆうと さくら Yuuto さとう Sakura Satoo Yui すずき Suzuki かいと Kaito ゆい たかはし Takahashi そうた Soota あおい Aoi Tanaka たなか りh Rin はるき Haruki Itoo いとう だいすけ かな Kana Daisuke Most Japanese names are written in kanji. For example, Tanaka is usually written as 田中, which means "middle of the rice feld." Family names are often related to nature or geographical features. Because many kanji share the same reading, names with the same pronunciation may be written with different kanji, such as 裕子 and 僵子 for the feminine name Yuuko.

contentBlocks.title: Lesson Overview
contentBlocks.text: New Friends introduces self-introductions and basic questions about names, school year, nationality, major, age, time, and phone numbers.

contentBlocks.title: Dialogue I: School Orientation
contentBlocks.text: Mary meets Takeshi at a school orientation. The exchange models greetings, stating a name, asking whether someone is an international student, nationality, and school year.

contentBlocks.title: Dialogue II: Self-Introduction
contentBlocks.text: Mary introduces herself to a group using name, school, year, major, age, and a closing greeting.

contentBlocks.title: Core Vocabulary
contentBlocks.text: Core Lesson 1 vocabulary covers school words, people, time, nationality, language, age, phone, and number expressions.

contentBlocks.title: Additional Vocabulary
contentBlocks.text: Additional vocabulary covers countries, majors, occupations, and family terms relevant to personal introductions.

contentBlocks.title: Grammar 1: X は Y です
contentBlocks.text: Explains using です to identify or describe someone or something, and using は to mark the topic when needed.

contentBlocks.title: Grammar 2: Question Sentences
contentBlocks.text: Explains making yes/no questions with か and asking information questions with words such as なん, なんじ, なんさい, and なんねんせい.

contentBlocks.title: Grammar 3: Noun1 の Noun2
contentBlocks.text: Explains using の to connect two nouns for possession, affiliation, or specification, with the second noun as the main idea.

contentBlocks.title: Expression Notes
contentBlocks.text: Expression notes cover あのう, そうですか, number pronunciation, 先生, さん, and ways to refer to the person you are talking to.

contentBlocks.title: Culture Note: Japanese Names
contentBlocks.text: Culture note explaining Japanese name order, common naming patterns, and how names are often written in kanji.

contentBlocks.title: Practice: X は Y です
contentBlocks.text: Practice describing people by nationality, year, school, major, age, and occupation using X は Y です.

contentBlocks.title: Practice: Questions
contentBlocks.text: Practice asking and answering questions about nationality, school year, age, and personal details.

contentBlocks.title: Practice: Host Family Questions
contentBlocks.text: Practice answering questions about Mary's host family using family terms, occupations, and ages.

contentBlocks.title: Practice: の Phrases
contentBlocks.text: Practice translating and describing noun phrases with の, including school, language, and relationship expressions.

contentBlocks.title: Practice: Telephone Numbers
contentBlocks.text: Pair and group work for asking, confirming, and recording telephone numbers.

contentBlocks.title: Practice: Time
contentBlocks.text: Practice telling time and asking what time it is in different places.

contentBlocks.title: Review Exercises
contentBlocks.text: Review activities combine questions about name, age, nationality, school, hometown, role play, and self-introduction.

contentBlocks.title: Useful Expressions: Time
contentBlocks.text: Reference table for hours and minutes, including common time expressions used in Lesson 1.

exercises.label: Workbook Lesson 1 Grammar 1 I: Describe Takeshi
exercises.prompt: Look at Takeshi's profile and describe him in Japanese using X は Y です.

exercises.label: Workbook Lesson 1 Grammar 1 II: Translate Sentences
exercises.prompt: Translate sentences such as “Ms. Ogawa is Japanese,” “Mr. Takeda is a teacher,” and “I am an international student” using X は Y です.

exercises.label: Workbook Lesson 1 Grammar 2 I: Complete Conversations
exercises.prompt: Use the textbook chart to complete question-and-answer exchanges about Mary, Professor Yamashita, Takeshi, and Robert.

exercises.label: Workbook Lesson 1 Grammar 2 II: Ask the Right Questions
exercises.prompt: Write appropriate questions for answers such as よねんせいです and じゅうきゅうさいです.

exercises.label: Workbook Lesson 1 Grammar 3 I: A の B Phrases
exercises.prompt: Translate noun phrases into Japanese using A の B, including Takeshi's telephone number, my friend, English-language teacher, Yui's major, and high school teacher.

exercises.label: Workbook Lesson 1 Grammar 3 II: Translate Sentences
exercises.prompt: Translate sentences involving majors, school affiliation, Japanese-language teachers, and Sakura University using の where appropriate.

exercises.label: Workbook Lesson 1 Time I: Write the Time
exercises.prompt: Look at the time prompts and answer いまなんじですか for four times.

exercises.label: Workbook Lesson 1 Telephone Numbers II: Interview Three People
exercises.prompt: Ask three people their phone numbers and write each number in Japanese and Arabic numerals.

exercises.label: Workbook Lesson 1 Questions: Answer in Japanese
exercises.prompt: Answer personal questions in Japanese about name, occupation, school year, age, major, and telephone number.

exercises.label: Workbook Lesson 1 Listening A: Picture Choice
exercises.prompt: Listen to phrases and choose the correct picture.

exercises.label: Workbook Lesson 1 Listening B: City Times
exercises.prompt: Listen to airplane dialogues and write the times for Paris, Seoul, New York, London, Taipei, and Sydney.

exercises.label: Workbook Lesson 1 Listening C: Phone Numbers
exercises.prompt: Listen to dialogues with a telephone operator and find the telephone numbers for Kawasaki, Lee, Woods, and Kumar.

exercises.label: Workbook Lesson 1 Listening D: True/False
exercises.prompt: Listen to Akira and Kate talking and mark each statement true or false.
